title: Grade 8 Ontario Financial Literacy Practice Packet
description: 30 questions modeled after Grade 8 financial literacy practice: income, net pay, budgeting, unit prices, discounts, Ontario HST, tips, simple interest, compound interest, profit, and multi-step money problems.

questions.title: Hourly income
questions.prompt: Maya earns \$12.50 per hour and works 16 hours in one week. What is her gross pay for the week?

options.feedback: This would be too low for 16 hours at \$12.50/hr.
Correct.
This does not match \(12.50 \times 16\).
This would be 20 hours at \$12.50/hr.

questions.hint: Multiply the hourly rate by the number of hours worked.

questions.title: Net income after deductions
questions.prompt: Jon earns \$640.00 in gross income. If 12% is deducted for taxes and other deductions, what is his net income?

options.feedback: Correct.
This is only the amount deducted.
This adds 12 instead of subtracting 12%.
This adds the deductions instead of subtracting them.

questions.hint: First find 12% of 640, then subtract it from 640.

questions.title: Monthly income from yearly income
questions.prompt: A part-time job pays \$36,000.00 per year. What is the average monthly income?

options.feedback: This would be \$28,800 per year.
Correct.
This divides by 10, not 12.
This multiplies by 12 instead of dividing by 12.

questions.hint: A year has 12 months.

questions.title: Commission income
questions.prompt: A salesperson earns 6% commission on a \$850.00 sale. How much commission do they earn?

options.feedback: This is 0.6% of the sale, not 6%.
Correct.
This is close, but not \(850 \times 0.06\).
This adds the commission to the sale amount.

questions.hint: Change 6% to 0.06 before multiplying.

questions.title: Budget surplus or deficit
questions.prompt: A student earns \$95.00. They spend \$18.00 on transportation, \$32.00 on food, \$20.00 on clothes, and \$15.00 on entertainment. How much money is left?

options.feedback: Check the total expenses again.
Correct.
This is the total expenses, not the amount left.
This adds income and expenses together.

questions.hint: Add all expenses first, then subtract from the income.

questions.title: Savings as a percent of income
questions.prompt: Priya earns \$300.00 from tutoring and saves \$45.00. What percent of her income does she save?

options.feedback: This would be \$30.00 saved.
Correct.
This uses the savings number as the percent.
This is not the savings percent.

questions.hint: Divide 45 by 300, then multiply by 100.

questions.title: Unit price
questions.prompt: A box of 12 granola bars costs \$8.40. What is the cost per granola bar?

options.feedback: This would make 12 bars cost \$7.20.
Correct.
This is not \(8.40 \div 12\).
This is too high for 12 bars costing \$8.40 total.

questions.hint: Divide the total cost by the number of bars.

questions.title: Best buy using unit rates
questions.prompt: Which bag of rice has the lowest price per 100 g?

options.feedback: This is \$0.75 per 100 g.
Correct.
This is \$0.79 per 100 g.
This is \$0.84 per 100 g.

questions.hint: Convert each option to price per 100 g.
solution: The best buy is 750 g for \$5.40.

questions.title: Sale discount
questions.prompt: A jacket costs \$80.00 and is 25% off. What is the sale price before tax?

options.feedback: This is only the discount amount.
Correct.
This adds 25% instead of subtracting it.

questions.hint: Find 25% of 80, then subtract it from 80.
solution: The sale price before tax is \$60.00.

questions.title: Ontario HST
questions.prompt: A backpack costs \$125.00 before tax. If Ontario HST is 13%, what is the total cost after tax?

options.feedback: This is only the HST amount.
This adds \$13.00 instead of 13%.
Correct.
This uses the wrong tax amount.

questions.hint: Find 13% of 125, then add it to 125.

questions.title: Discount and tax
questions.prompt: A video game costs \$64.99 and is 20% off. After the discount, Ontario HST of 13% is added. What is the final cost, rounded to the nearest cent?

options.feedback: This is the discounted price before tax.
Correct.
This is close to subtracting the discount after tax incorrectly.
This adds tax but does not apply the discount.

questions.hint: Take off 20% first, then multiply by 1.13 for HST.
solution: Rounded to the nearest cent, the final cost is \$58.75.

questions.title: Working backwards from tax-included price
questions.prompt: A tax-included total is \$113.00. If the HST rate is 13%, what was the price before tax?

options.feedback: This is the tax amount on \$100.00, not the before-tax price.
Correct.
This adds another 13% instead of removing tax.
This multiplies by 1.13 instead of dividing by 1.13.

questions.hint: A price with 13% tax included is 113% of the original price.

questions.title: Tip calculation
questions.prompt: A restaurant meal costs \$42.00 before tip. If you leave a 15% tip, what is the total cost?

options.feedback: This is only the tip.
This uses 10% instead of 15%.
Correct.
This adds 15 dollars instead of 15%.

questions.hint: Find 15% of 42, then add it to 42.

questions.title: Simple interest earned
questions.prompt: Nora invests \$600.00 at a simple interest rate of 4% per year for 3 years. How much interest does she earn?

options.feedback: This is the interest for only 1 year.
Correct.
This is the total after 1 year, not interest after 3 years.
This is the final balance, not the interest earned.

questions.hint: Use \(I = Prt\) with \(r=0.04\) and \(t=3\).

questions.title: Simple interest total payback
questions.prompt: Omar borrows \$1,200.00 at a simple interest rate of 5% per year for 2 years. What total amount must he pay back?

options.feedback: This is only the interest.
This is only one year of interest added.
Correct.
This doubles the principal without using the rate.

questions.hint: Find the interest first, then add it to the principal.

questions.title: Finding principal from simple interest
questions.prompt: Sophia wants to earn \$90.00 in simple interest over 2 years at a rate of 3% per year. What principal amount must she invest?

options.feedback: This multiplies the values instead of dividing by the rate and time.
Check the rate and time calculation.
Correct.
This forgets to include the 2 years correctly.

questions.hint: Use \(P=I/(rt)\), where \(r=0.03\) and \(t=2\).

questions.title: Compound interest
questions.prompt: Emma invests \$1,000.00 at an interest rate of 5% per year, compounded annually. What will her balance be after 2 years?

options.feedback: This is the balance after only 1 year.
This is simple interest for 2 years.
Correct.
This is only the compound interest earned, not the total balance.

questions.hint: For compound interest, the second year earns interest on the first year's new balance.

questions.title: Simple vs compound interest
questions.prompt: A \$1,000.00 investment earns 4% per year for 2 years. How much more money does compound interest earn than simple interest, if it is compounded annually?

options.feedback: Compound and simple interest are not always equal after more than one compounding period.
Correct.
This is one year of interest.
This is the total compound interest earned, not the difference.

questions.hint: Find the simple-interest total and compound-interest total, then subtract.

questions.title: Saving toward a goal
questions.prompt: Leo wants to save \$360.00 in 9 months. He already has \$90.00 saved. How much does he need to save each month?

options.feedback: Correct.
This forgets that he already has \$90.00 saved.
Check the amount remaining and number of months.
This is the total amount still needed, not the monthly amount.

questions.hint: Subtract what he already saved before dividing by 9.
solution: Leo needs to save \$30.00 each month.

questions.title: Percent increase
questions.prompt: A student's weekly allowance increases from \$20.00 to \$25.00. What is the percent increase?

options.feedback: This is the dollar increase, not the percent increase.
This compares the increase to the new amount.
Correct.
This is the new amount as a percent of the old amount.

questions.hint: The increase is \$5.00. Compare that to the original \$20.00.

questions.title: Profit after expenses
questions.prompt: A student sells handmade bracelets and earns \$240.00 in revenue. They spend \$75.00 on materials and \$25.00 for a table at a craft fair. What is their profit?

options.feedback: This is the total expenses, not the profit.
Correct.
This subtracts only the materials.
This adds expenses to revenue instead of subtracting them.

questions.hint: Add the expenses first, then subtract from revenue.

questions.title: Net profit from a contract
questions.prompt: A freelancer takes a contract to build a website for \$3,200.00. They spend \$450.00 on software licences and hire an assistant for 10 hours at \$25.00/hr. What is the freelancer's net profit from the contract?

options.feedback: Correct.
This subtracts the assistant cost but forgets the software licence cost.
This subtracts only part of the expenses.
This is the revenue before expenses.

questions.hint: Assistant cost is \(10 \times 25\), then subtract both expenses from revenue.

questions.title: Take-home pay
questions.prompt: A student earns \$15.00 per hour and works 20 hours. If 8% is deducted, what is the take-home pay?

options.feedback: This is only the amount deducted.
Correct.
This subtracts 8 dollars instead of 8%.
This is the gross pay before deductions.

questions.hint: Find gross pay first, then subtract 8% of it.

questions.title: Markup percent
questions.prompt: A store buys headphones for \$30.00 and sells them for \$42.00. What is the markup as a percent of the store's cost?

options.feedback: This is the dollar markup written as a percent.
This compares the markup to the selling price, not the cost.
Correct.
This is the selling price as a percent of cost, not the markup percent.

questions.hint: First find the dollar markup, then divide by the cost.
solution: The markup is 40% of the cost.

questions.title: Comparing simple interest offers
questions.prompt: Two savings accounts use simple interest. Account A pays 3% per year on \$800.00 for 2 years. Account B pays 4% per year on \$600.00 for 2 years. Which account earns more interest, and by how much?

options.feedback: They do not earn the same amount.
This is Account A's interest, not the difference.
This is Account B's interest, not the difference.

options.text: Neither; they earn the same interest
options.feedback: Correct.

questions.hint: Calculate \(I=Prt\) for both accounts.
solution: They both earn \$48.00, so neither earns more.

questions.title: Taxable and non-taxable items
questions.prompt: A sweater costs \$48.00 before tax. A snack costs \$4.50 and is not taxed in this situation. If 13% HST is charged only on the sweater, what is the total cost?

options.feedback: This adds the two prices but forgets HST on the sweater.
This applies HST to the snack only.
Correct.
This applies HST to both the sweater and the snack.

questions.hint: Only calculate HST on \$48.00, then add the snack price.

questions.title: Monthly plan with tax
questions.prompt: A phone plan costs \$38.00 per month before tax. Ontario HST is 13%. What is the total cost for 6 months after tax?

options.feedback: This is the cost for 1 month after tax.
This is the cost for 6 months before tax.
Correct.
This uses the wrong tax calculation.

questions.hint: Find one month after tax, then multiply by 6.

questions.title: Budget table
questions.prompt: Use the monthly budget table. Is the budget a surplus or a deficit, and by how much?

options.feedback: Check the total expenses.
Correct.
This adds expenses to income.
This is the total expenses, not the deficit amount.

questions.hint: Add every expense category, including savings, then compare to income.
solution: The budget has a deficit of \$35.00.

questions.title: Multiple discounts and tax
questions.prompt: A pair of shoes costs \$120.00. The store gives 30% off, then an extra 10% off the sale price. After both discounts, 13% HST is added. What is the final cost?

options.feedback: This is the price after both discounts before tax.
Correct.
This treats the discounts as one 40% discount, then adds tax.
This adds tax to the original price without applying discounts correctly.

questions.hint: Do the discounts one at a time, then add tax at the end.
solution: Rounded to the nearest cent, the final cost is \$85.43.

questions.title: Savings rate after expenses
questions.prompt: A student earns \$420.00 in a month. They spend \$135.00 on food, \$48.00 on transit, and \$57.00 on entertainment. They save all the money left over. What percent of their income do they save?

options.feedback: Correct.
This is the percent spent, not saved.
This is the amount saved written as a percent incorrectly.
This is the total expenses written as a percent incorrectly.

questions.hint: Find total expenses, subtract from income, then convert savings to a percent of income.
solution: Rounded to one decimal place, the student saves 42.9% of their income.
